questions.question: Consider the first-order logic sentence phi = exists s exists t exists u forall v forall w exists x forall y psi(s,t,u,v,w,x,y), where psi(s,t,u,v,w,x,y) is a quantifier-free first-order logic formula using only predicate symbols, and possibly equality, but no function symbols. Suppose phi has a model with a universe containing 7 elements. Which one of the following statements is necessarily true? A. There exists at least one model of phi with universe of size less than or equal to 3   B. There exists no model of phi with universe of size less than or equal to 3   C. There exists no model of phi with universe size of greater than 7   D. Every model of phi has a universe of size equal to 7
questions.answer: A. There exists at least one model of phi with universe of size less than or equal to 3
questions.to_find: Which statement about the model sizes of phi is necessarily true

understand.plain: The sentence phi is in prenex form: read the quantifier prefix exists s exists t exists u forall v forall w exists x forall y. To build a satisfying universe you only ever need elements that the existential quantifiers actually demand, because universally quantified variables can be made to reuse those same elements. Counting the leading/free existentials gives a tiny universe that still works.

keywords.explain: A formula written with all quantifiers pulled to the front, followed by a quantifier-free matrix psi. The order of the prefix is what controls dependencies between variables.
keywords.example: exists s exists t exists u forall v forall w exists x forall y psi(...) is already in prenex form.

keywords.explain: Each existential quantifier only needs one witness element to be true; universals can be instantiated by reusing existing witnesses, so the model need not be large.
keywords.example: The three leading exists s, exists t, exists u demand at most 3 distinct elements; the inner exists x can be a function of v, w but can still map into that 3-element set.

keywords.explain: A claim that holds for every situation consistent with the premises, not just the given 7-element model.
keywords.example: Here we must pick the option that is forced no matter what psi is.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='20' y='30'>Prefix: exists s, exists t, exists u | forall v forall w | exists x | forall y</text><rect class='hi' x='40' y='70' width='60' height='60' rx='8'/><text class='ink lbl' x='62' y='107'>s</text><rect class='hi' x='120' y='70' width='60' height='60' rx='8'/><text class='ink lbl' x='142' y='107'>t</text><rect class='hi' x='200' y='70' width='60' height='60' rx='8'/><text class='ink lbl' x='222' y='107'>u</text><text class='ok lbl' x='300' y='107'>3 witnesses suffice</text><text class='mut sub' x='40' y='165'>universe of size <= 3 can model phi (universals reuse s,t,u)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The three leading existential quantifiers need only three witness elements, so a universe of size at most 3 can satisfy phi.

given.aim: Decide which statement about model sizes of phi must hold given that one 7-element model exists.

terms.meaning: The prenex sentence exists s exists t exists u forall v forall w exists x forall y psi(...).
terms.example: Its prefix begins with three existentials.
terms.connects: Its existential count bounds the smallest possible model.

terms.meaning: A quantifier-free matrix with only predicates and equality, no function symbols.
terms.example: psi could assert equalities/predicates among the seven variables.
terms.connects: Because there are no functions, witnesses can collapse onto few elements.

terms.meaning: The set of elements over which the quantifiers range.
terms.connects: We ask how small/large a universe can be for phi to stay true.

terms.meaning: The number of elements in the universe of a model.
terms.example: Sizes 3, 7, or larger are all under consideration.
terms.connects: The leading existentials cap the minimal size at the count of independent witnesses.

given.plan: Use the bounded-model idea: a prenex sentence whose prefix forces only k independent existential witnesses has a model of size at most k. Count the witnesses to find a guaranteed small model and reject claims about forbidden small or capped large sizes.

steps.title: Count the existential witnesses
steps.formula_raw: minimal model size <= (number of existential variables not governed by a preceding forall that they must depend on)
steps.apply: The prefix is exists s exists t exists u forall v forall w exists x forall y. The first three (s, t, u) are unconditional witnesses; the inner exists x depends on v, w but its value may be chosen inside the already-fixed set. The forall variables (v, w, y) need no new elements.
steps.note: Only s, t, u are forced to be new, distinct-or-not witnesses, giving at most 3 essential elements.

steps.title: Build a model of size <= 3
steps.formula_raw: collapse all elements onto the witness set {s,t,u}
steps.apply: Take the 7-element model and map every element onto the chosen s, t, u. Since psi has no function symbols, predicate truth can be preserved by identifying elements; the universally quantified v, w, y simply range over these <= 3 elements, and x is picked among them. This yields a model with universe size <= 3.
steps.note: Hence option A (there exists a model of size <= 3) is forced; B is false. Sizes larger than 7 are not forbidden, so C is false, and uniqueness of size 7 (D) is false.

solution.result: Option A: there exists at least one model of phi with universe of size less than or equal to 3.

questions.formula_note: The bounded-model property of prenex first-order sentences is key: with no function symbols, only the leading existential witnesses (here s, t, u) force new elements, so a model of size at most 3 always exists.

questions.question: Consider the first order predicate formula phi: forall x [ (forall z  z|x  =>  ((z = x) v (z = 1)) )  ->  exists w (w > x) ^ (forall z  z|w  =>  ((w = z) v (z = 1)) ) ]. Here a|b denotes that a divides b, where a and b are integers. Consider the following sets:  S1: {1, 2, 3, ..., 100}   S2: Set of all positive integers   S3: Set of all integers. Which of the above sets satisfy phi? A. S1 and S2   B. S1 and S3   C. S2 and S3   D. S1, S2 and S3
questions.to_find: Which of the sets S1, S2, S3 satisfy the formula phi

understand.plain: The inner condition (forall z, z|x => z=x or z=1) says x is prime (or 1). So phi reads: for every prime x there is a larger prime w. That is exactly the statement 'there are infinitely many primes'. Over an infinite set of integers this is true, but over the finite set {1,...,100} it fails because the largest prime there (97) has no larger prime inside the set.

keywords.explain: forall z (z|x => z=x or z=1) means the only divisors of x are 1 and x, i.e. x is prime (1 also vacuously qualifies).
keywords.example: x = 7: divisors are 1 and 7 only, so the antecedent holds.

keywords.explain: phi asserts that for any prime x there exists a strictly larger prime w; this holds in all integers but not in a bounded finite range.
keywords.example: After the prime 97, S1 = {1,...,100} contains no larger prime, breaking phi.

keywords.explain: A set satisfies phi when the formula is true when its quantifiers range over that set.
keywords.example: S2 (all positive integers) makes phi true; S1 makes it false.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='20' y='30'>phi: for every prime x, some larger prime w exists</text><text class='ink lbl' x='20' y='70'>S1 = {1..100}:</text><text class='no lbl' x='180' y='70'>97 prime, no larger prime <= 100  -> FALSE</text><text class='ink lbl' x='20' y='110'>S2 = positive ints:</text><text class='ok lbl' x='210' y='110'>infinitely many primes -> TRUE</text><text class='ink lbl' x='20' y='150'>S3 = all integers:</text><text class='ok lbl' x='200' y='150'>still infinitely many primes -> TRUE</text><text class='mut sub' x='20' y='185'>Answer: S2 and S3 satisfy phi</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: phi says every prime has a larger prime; this is true for the infinite sets S2 and S3 but false for the finite set S1 capped at 100.

given.aim: Determine for which of S1, S2, S3 the sentence phi is true.

terms.meaning: a divides b (b is a multiple of a).
terms.connects: Used to express the divisor condition that characterizes primes.

terms.meaning: forall z (z|x => z = x or z = 1), i.e. x has no divisors other than 1 and itself.
terms.connects: Triggers the requirement that a larger prime w exists.

terms.meaning: exists w with w > x and w prime.
terms.connects: Fails in a bounded finite domain after the last prime.

terms.meaning: S1 finite {1..100}; S2 positive integers; S3 all integers.
terms.example: S1 has a maximum element 100; S2 and S3 are unbounded.
terms.connects: Unboundedness is exactly what lets phi hold.

given.plan: Translate phi into 'every prime has a larger prime' (infinitude of primes), then test each domain: it fails on the bounded S1 (max prime 97) and holds on the unbounded S2 and S3.

steps.title: Decode the divisor condition as 'prime'
steps.formula_raw: forall z ( z|n => (z = n) v (z = 1) )  <=>  n is prime (or 1)
steps.apply: The antecedent forall z (z|x => z=x or z=1) is true exactly when x is prime (1 qualifies vacuously). The bracket inside exists w similarly says w is prime. So phi becomes: forall prime x, exists prime w with w > x.
steps.note: phi is the statement 'there is no largest prime'.

steps.title: Evaluate phi on each domain
steps.formula_raw: phi true  <=>  domain contains arbitrarily large primes
steps.apply: S1 = {1,...,100}: the prime x = 97 has no larger prime w <= 100, so phi is FALSE. S2 = positive integers: by Euclid there are infinitely many primes, so for every prime x a larger prime w exists -> phi TRUE. S3 = all integers: the positive primes are still present and unbounded above, so phi TRUE.
steps.note: Negative integers do not break phi since the witness w only needs to be a larger prime, which exists among the positives.

solution.result: Only S2 and S3 satisfy phi, so the answer is C.

questions.formula_note: Recognizing the divisor clause as the definition of a prime turns phi into 'infinitely many primes', which holds on the unbounded S2 and S3 but fails on the bounded S1.

questions.question: Which one of the following predicate formulae is NOT logically valid? Note that W is a predicate formula without any free occurrence of x. A. forall x (p(x) v W) == forall x (p(x)) v W   B. exists x (p(x) ^ W) == exists x (p(x)) ^ W   C. forall x (p(x) -> W) == forall x (p(x)) -> W   D. exists x (p(x) -> W) == forall x (p(x)) -> W
questions.answer: C. forall x (p(x) -> W) == forall x (p(x)) -> W
questions.to_find: Which quantifier-distribution equivalence is NOT logically valid

understand.plain: When W has no free x, quantifiers can be pushed in or pulled out across connectives, but the direction matters. forall distributes over v with a constant W, and exists distributes over ^ with a constant W; these are valid. For implication, because the antecedent flips the quantifier (an antecedent under forall behaves like exists), the equivalence in C is wrong. Option D is the correct implication law.

keywords.term: Quantifier scope with a constant W
keywords.explain: If W has no free x, then forall x W == W and exists x W == W, letting W move in or out of a quantifier's scope.
keywords.example: forall x (p(x) v W) lets W leave the scope: == (forall x p(x)) v W.

keywords.explain: forall x (p(x) -> W) == (exists x p(x)) -> W, because A -> W = not A v W and forall over a negation becomes exists; the antecedent quantifier inverts.
keywords.example: forall x (p(x) -> W) is NOT the same as (forall x p(x)) -> W.

keywords.explain: An equivalence that holds in every interpretation; the NOT-valid one fails in some model.
keywords.example: C fails when some p(x) holds, all do not, and W is false.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='16' y='28'>W has no free x</text><text class='ok sub' x='16' y='62'>A: forall x (p(x) v W) == (forall x p(x)) v W   valid</text><text class='ok sub' x='16' y='90'>B: exists x (p(x) ^ W) == (exists x p(x)) ^ W   valid</text><text class='no sub' x='16' y='118'>C: forall x (p(x) -> W) == (forall x p(x)) -> W   NOT valid</text><text class='ok sub' x='16' y='146'>D: exists x (p(x) -> W) == (forall x p(x)) -> W   valid</text><text class='mut sub' x='16' y='184'>Counter to C: some p(x)=T, not all p=T, W=F : LHS=F, RHS=T</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three equivalences (A, B, D) are valid FOL quantifier-distribution laws; C is not, because the antecedent of an implication inverts the quantifier.

given.aim: Identify the single equivalence that is not a logical validity.

terms.connects: Its quantification interacts with the constant W.

terms.meaning: A predicate formula with no free occurrence of x (acts as a constant w.r.t. x).
terms.example: W could be 'q(y)' or a closed sentence.
terms.connects: Because x is not free in W, W can cross the quantifier boundary.

terms.example: p(x) -> W is not p(x) v W.
terms.connects: Negating the antecedent flips forall to exists, breaking C.

terms.example: A, B, D hold universally; C does not.
terms.connects: We choose the non-valid equivalence as the answer.

given.plan: Apply the standard quantifier-distribution laws (W constant in x), rewriting implications as not-antecedent OR consequent, and find which stated equivalence reverses a quantifier incorrectly.

steps.title: Validate A, B, D using constant-W laws
steps.formula_raw: forall x (p(x) v W) == (forall x p(x)) v W ;  exists x (p(x) ^ W) == (exists x p(x)) ^ W ;  forall x (p(x) -> W) == (exists x p(x)) -> W
steps.apply: A and B are direct distribution laws of forall over v and exists over ^ when W has no free x; both valid. For D, rewrite exists x (p(x) -> W) = exists x (not p(x) v W) = (exists x not p(x)) v W = not(forall x p(x)) v W = (forall x p(x)) -> W; valid.
steps.note: All three reduce to known equivalences.

steps.title: Show C fails by a counterexample
steps.formula_raw: forall x (p(x) -> W) == (exists x p(x)) -> W   (correct law), so C using forall is wrong
steps.apply: The correct law is forall x (p(x) -> W) == (exists x p(x)) -> W, NOT (forall x p(x)) -> W. Counterexample for C: domain {a,b} with p(a) true, p(b) false, W false. LHS forall x (p(x) -> W): for x=a, p(a)->W = (T->F) = F, so LHS = F. RHS (forall x p(x)) -> W = (F) -> F = T. Since F != T, C is not valid.
steps.note: C confuses the antecedent quantifier; it should become exists, not forall.

solution.result: Option C is the formula that is NOT logically valid.

questions.formula_note: The key law is forall x (p(x) -> W) == (exists x p(x)) -> W; option C wrongly keeps forall on the antecedent side, so it is the invalid equivalence.

questions.question:
Geetha has a conjecture about integers, which is of the form:
forall z(P(z)) => exists y(Q(y, y))
where P is a statement about integers, and Q is a statement about pairs of integers.

Which of the following (one or more) options imply Geetha's conjecture?

A. forall p(P(p) ^ ~Q(p, p))
B. forall p(P(p) -> Q(p, p))
C. exists p(P(p) -> Q(p, p))
D. exists p(P(p) ^ ~Q(p, p))
questions.to_find: Which options logically imply the conjecture forall z P(z) => exists y Q(y,y)

understand.plain: Geetha's conjecture says: if P holds for every integer, then there exists some integer y such that Q(y,y) holds (y relates to itself under Q). To imply this conjecture, an option must guarantee that whenever all integers have property P, some integer y satisfies Q(y,y). Option B says every integer satisfies P->Q(p,p), so if all integers have P then all satisfy Q(p,p), giving an easy witness. Option C says at least one integer p0 satisfies P(p0)->Q(p0,p0); if we also know forall z P(z), then P(p0) is true, forcing Q(p0,p0), so exists y Q(y,y) holds.

keywords.explain: Option X implies the conjecture when every situation (interpretation) where X is true is also a situation where the conjecture is true.
keywords.example: B implies the conjecture because B being true forces Q(p,p) for all p, giving the needed witness.

keywords.explain: Q is a two-argument predicate; Q(y,y) means y relates to itself. The conjecture requires at least one such y.
keywords.example: If Q(x,y) means 'x and y are coprime', Q(y,y) means 'y is coprime with itself', i.e. y = 1.

keywords.explain: exists p(P(p)->Q(p,p)) is true if some p0 satisfies P(p0)->Q(p0,p0). When P holds everywhere, P(p0) is true, making Q(p0,p0) forced by the implication.
keywords.example: Option C picks one witness; when forall z P(z) is added, that witness becomes effective.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='16' y='28'>Conjecture: forall z P(z) => exists y Q(y,y)</text><text class='no sub' x='16' y='64'>A: forall p(P(p) ^ ~Q(p,p))  P holds everywhere but ~Q holds too -> conjecture FAILS</text><text class='ok sub' x='16' y='92'>B: forall p(P(p) -> Q(p,p))  if forall z P(z) then forall p Q(p,p) -> implies</text><text class='ok sub' x='16' y='120'>C: exists p(P(p) -> Q(p,p))  one p0: P(p0) forced true, so Q(p0,p0) -> implies</text><text class='no sub' x='16' y='148'>D: exists p(P(p) ^ ~Q(p,p))  some p has P and ~Q -> not guaranteed to imply</text><text class='mut sub' x='16' y='190'>Answer: B and C both imply Geetha conjecture</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Options B (universal P->Q(p,p)) and C (one witness p0 with P->Q(p0,p0)) both imply the conjecture; A directly contradicts it; D is inconclusive.

given.aim: Identify which of A-D logically imply the conjecture forall z P(z) => exists y Q(y,y).

terms.term: Conjecture: forall z P(z) => exists y Q(y,y)
terms.meaning: If every integer has property P, then at least one integer y has the reflexive property Q(y,y).
terms.example: If P(n) means 'n is odd' and Q(a,b) means 'a+b is even', then Q(y,y) means y+y=2y is even -- always true for any y, so conjecture holds.
terms.connects: An option implies the conjecture if it forces exists y Q(y,y) whenever all integers have P.

terms.meaning: Every integer has P AND lacks Q(p,p). This makes forall z P(z) true but forces NO y to have Q(y,y).
terms.example: A makes the conjecture's antecedent true but the consequent false, so A contradicts the conjecture.
terms.connects: A is the direct negation of the existential conclusion combined with the universal premise.

terms.meaning: For every integer p, if P(p) then Q(p,p). Combined with forall z P(z), every integer has Q(p,p).
terms.example: B is strictly stronger than the conjecture; B => conjecture trivially.
terms.connects: B gives a universal witness -- every p qualifies as y.

terms.meaning: Some specific integer p0 satisfies P(p0)->Q(p0,p0). When forall z P(z) holds, P(p0) is true, forcing Q(p0,p0).
terms.example: C picks one p0; the assumption forall z P(z) activates the implication to give Q(p0,p0).
terms.connects: C implies the conjecture through the specific witness p0.

given.plan: For each option X, check whether X ^ (forall z P(z)) |= exists y Q(y,y). For A: it directly falsifies the consequent. For B: universal modus ponens yields forall p Q(p,p), hence the existential. For C: the single witness p0 with P(p0)->Q(p0,p0) gives Q(p0,p0) once forall z P(z) is assumed. For D: one P-element with ~Q(p,p) does not preclude exists y Q(y,y) elsewhere, so D is insufficient.

steps.title: Option A contradicts the conjecture
steps.formula_raw: R implies C iff every model of R is also a model of C.
steps.apply: If A = forall p(P(p) ^ ~Q(p,p)) is true, then forall z P(z) is true (from the P(p) part). Also, ~Q(p,p) holds for every p, so NO y satisfies Q(y,y). The conjecture's conclusion exists y Q(y,y) is false while its antecedent forall z P(z) is true. The conjecture becomes True => False = False. Option A does NOT imply the conjecture; it refutes it.
steps.note: A is the worst case: all P holds everywhere but Q(y,y) fails everywhere.

steps.title: Option B implies the conjecture
steps.formula_raw: From forall x(A(x)->B(x)) and A(a), conclude B(a).
steps.apply: Assume B = forall p(P(p)->Q(p,p)) and assume the conjecture's antecedent forall z P(z). By universal instantiation, for any integer p0, P(p0) is true. By modus ponens on B applied to p0: Q(p0,p0) is true. So exists y Q(y,y) holds (choosing y = p0). The conjecture is satisfied. Option B implies the conjecture.
steps.note: B is strictly stronger: it gives Q(p,p) for EVERY p, not just one.

steps.title: Option C implies the conjecture
steps.formula_raw: If P(p0) is true and P(p0)->Q(p0,p0) is true, then Q(p0,p0) is true.
steps.apply: Assume C = exists p(P(p)->Q(p,p)). Let p0 be the witness: P(p0)->Q(p0,p0) holds. Now assume forall z P(z). Then P(p0) is true. By modus ponens: Q(p0,p0) is true. So exists y Q(y,y) holds (y = p0). The conjecture is satisfied. Option C implies the conjecture.
steps.note: The key: when forall z P(z) is given, the implication P(p0)->Q(p0,p0) is no longer vacuous -- P(p0) is true, so Q(p0,p0) must hold.

steps.title: Option D does not imply the conjecture
steps.apply: Assume D = exists p(P(p) ^ ~Q(p,p)). This says some p0 has P(p0)=true and Q(p0,p0)=false. Does this force the conjecture? No. Consider: domain = {p0, p1}. P(p0)=T, Q(p0,p0)=F, P(p1)=F, Q(p1,p1)=F. Then D is true (p0 witnesses it). Forall z P(z) is false (P(p1)=F), so the conjecture's antecedent is false, making the conjecture vacuously true. But we could also have: P(p0)=T, P(p1)=T, Q(p0,p0)=F, Q(p1,p1)=F. Then D is true (p0), forall z P(z) is true, but exists y Q(y,y) is false. Conjecture fails. So D does NOT imply the conjecture.
steps.note: D only guarantees one element with P and ~Q, leaving the other elements unconstrained.

solution.result: B, C. Options B and C both logically imply Geetha's conjecture forall z P(z) => exists y Q(y,y).

questions.formula_note: fol-implication-validity is the core check: B implies the conjecture by universally forcing Q(p,p) for every P-element; C implies it by using a single witness p0 that becomes effective once forall z P(z) is given as context.
